content:
A feature request mentioned in the discussion after Nina Otter's talk is to make admins/moderators more visible, so that people know who to contact if someone is displaying inappropriate behaviour.
On a related note, I would like to know what conventions we can agree upon about what should be done when:

  1. Someone is being (or coming across as) aggressive, or otherwise violating the intended spirit of this forum. Should a moderator simply make their presence felt in requesting a change of tone? If that fails or things return to ugly, should messages be deleted? Should such conversations be moved to a private stream for conflict resolution!?
  2. A small number of people are dominating a topic that would benefit from more varied contributions. This has happened a bunch of times, notably in #practice: communication. The discussions have generally been interesting to read, so I have not acted to take them down, but at a certain point the volume of text must be offputting to newcomers to the conversation. Perhaps a conciseness guideline would be valuable? I'll suggest this in #general: values too.

Depending on how big the community gets, moderators should not be expected to follow every discussion. If possible, it might be better to have a flagging mechanism for anyone to raise a (privately issued) flag to the moderator team for attention, with a brief description of the problem. That's how it works at the StackExchange sites like MathOverflow.

That would work if and when the scale increases, but I like the idea of people knowing individual people that they can discuss a problem/issue with. At the moment, as John Baez noted, the scale is small enough that the active users could in principle know and recognise one another personally, and I would like to foster that sense of community for as long as it remains feasible.
Actually, I think it would work regardless of the size, but also that the current size doesn't make it urgent.

Regarding 1, a very common practice on the internet is to warn users that are acting disrespectfully/inappropriately. After a couple of warnings, if nothing changes, the user is banned, either temporarily or permanently
Obviously some forms of behavior (e.g. manifest racism, or other behaviors openly and totally violating the code of conduct) are usually dealt with a direct permaban

A very nice feature that IRC had but Zulip seems to lack is "mute". It basically allowed to forbid a user to send messages, making them only able to read. In some situation it's very useful to temporarily mute someone, e.g. when this someone takes over a conversation completely.

That would be nice. As far as I can tell, there is no feature enabling a "soft/temporary ban" at the moment, so that would be handy to have. A feature which has been mentioned and would be useful under other circumstances is the ability to lock a particular discussion (temporarily, say), to give some enforced cool-down time. For example, if two participants are equally at fault when a discussion gets out of hand, to be able to force them to cool off with a time out might be handy.

One feature I wish we could have is the ability for any person to mute any other person, so that people who don't get along can ignore each other. In Twitter this makes it possible for me to have a very happy friendly experience despite even though there's very limited moderation. Actually Twitter has both "mute" and the more severe "block".
Wouldn't that just result in people talking across one another without realising it here?
Yeah, it works great on twitter but twitter threads are trees and zulip threads are lists, I'm not sure it would work so well here

I'm not really in favor of mute. If there's more of a rabble (think J. Rando who knows nothing about category theory and has come to mouth off), then that would be one thing. But in this chat it should be possible for participants to find ways of getting along, sometimes with moderator assistance. By the way, who are the moderators here, just so I know? Morgan, Christian, and John B. are the names I can think of off the top of my head.
Maybe the mods could all add '[Mod]' to the end of their usernames?
Then you could see they were a mod when they posted and also in the list of users
I've added it at the start of my username, good idea until any feature gets implemented :working_on_it:
So to be clear, I am an administrator according to zulip. I don't know if I want to also be a moderator

That's fair. Mods definitely need admin powers, but admins don't all need mod responsibilities :joy:
We can always recruit in the near future, especially whenever there is a spike in numbers after the first post-pandemic in-person CT conference.
I vaguely remember having this conversation in the first days after Christian set the thing up

I'm happy to be a moderator until enough bureaucracy builds up that I find it intolerable. At a certain point in the growth of a community, there get to be a bunch of people who spend a bunch of time talking about how things are working - that's what I mean by "bureaucracy".

Jules Hedges said:

So to be clear, I am an administrator according to zulip. I don't know if I want to also be a moderator

The role you originally volunteered for was "moderator", though. Zulip doesn't distinguish between the two notions internally.
That is, there's a distinction between the social role of a moderator and administrator, but their powers are the same, because they're both "administrators" according to Zulip.

Nathanael Arkor said:

The role you originally volunteered for was "moderator", though. Zulip doesn't distinguish between the two notions internally.

You remember better than me... guess I'd better do my part then
I just remember everyone volunteering in #general: meta > moderators :grinning_face_with_smiling_eyes:
I don't think a moderator's role will be very intense.

Todd Trimble said:

I'm not really in favor of mute. If there's more of a rabble (think J. Rando who knows nothing about category theory and has come to mouth off), then that would be one thing. But in this chat it should be possible for participants to find ways of getting along, sometimes with moderator assistance. By the way, who are the moderators here, just so I know? Morgan, Christian, and John B. are the names I can think of off the top of my head.

Ahem. Could someone just please answer my question: who are the mods? I could scour the cited thread; on the other hand, it should only be 15 seconds of work for someone just to write the names.
I don't know who all the moderators are! We are in the early phases before the bureaucracy has been structured, obviously.
Let's see if I can find out who the moderators/administrators are.

So if I peek into the control panel, I see these folks listed as "moderators":

@Christian Williams
@Jules Hedges
@Matteo Capucci
@Nathanael Arkor
@Fabrizio Genovese

Not me, apparently! This is gonna make it easier for me to quit when I get tired. :+1:
Uh--that's a surprise.
Surprise! You're in charge! :scared:
I thought Christian had made me a moderator, and my ability to do things here suggests I am, so maybe this list is inaccurate somehow, even though it's there on the control panel.

It's totally ok for me, it's just weird since I don't recall to have volunteered (but my episodic memory is trash). My presence is extremely intermittent (actually, almost completely passive lately), so I can be a 'reserve' in case an extra hand is needed.

John Baez said:

So if I peek into the control panel, I see these folks listed as "moderators":

Christian Williams
Jules Hedges
Matteo Capucci
Nathanael Arkor
Fabrizio Genovese

Not me, apparently! This is gonna make it easier for me to quit when I get tired. :+1:

I was going to mention user groups; that's what you were looking at in the settings panel @John Baez. You can mention all the moderators in that user group with @*moderators* (type "@mod" and it'll be at the top of the autocomplete list). It's also possible to see the "Administrators" in the users list by sorting by role; they are currently:

Christian Williams
Fabrizio Genovese
John Baez
Jules Hedges
Nathanael Arkor
Stelios Tsampas
[Mod] Morgan Rogers

John Baez said:

I thought Christian had made me a moderator, and my ability to do things here suggests I am, so maybe this list is inaccurate somehow, even though it's there on the control panel.

That moderators user group is just a user group called "moderators"; it doesn't give any special powers. You are an Administrator, so you do have special powers.
(Anyone can create a user group by the way.)
Okay, I get it.
So do administrators equate with moderators?
Moderators are a distinct, custom user-group. I am assuming (we) admins have near-infinite permissions and they should subsume that of moderators.

@Christian Williams is the ultimate authority here, I think. All I know is what I just learned:

  • there's group of "administrators" with extra powers on this website, like the power to ban people etc. etc.

  • someone has created a user-group of "moderators", probably for the purposes of communicating with that group.
I have taken the liberty of putting myself in that user-group of "moderators", just to reduce confusion.
So, right now only @Stelios Tsampas is an "administrator" but not a "moderator".
Treachery!
:upside_down: Do you want to be a moderator? Does anyone not want Stelios to be a moderator?
We can use all we can get.
Yes, I don't remember who made that user-group but we'll be utilizing those roles more in the near future.
Oh, I am honored and moved by the invitation @John Baez ! :grinning_face_with_smiling_eyes:
